where: Tol Eressëa; Nísimaldar in Númenor; Lothlórien; one alone in the Party Field of Hobbiton
use: the flets (talan) of the Galadhrim were built in them; its leaves wrapped lembas
q: Mellyrn they are called, and are those that bear the yellow blossom, but I have never climbed in one.
note: Legolas speaks. The LotR index glosses 'Mallorn (Golden Tree, pl. mellyrn)'; UT's index gives 'Quenya malinornë, plural malinorni'. UT's Description of Númenor adds the fullest account: silver bark, upswept boughs, leaves pale green above and silver beneath turning gold in autumn, blossom in clusters like a cherry, fruit a nut with a silver shale. Christopher Tolkien notes 'This description of the mallorn is much like that given by Legolas'. The Lothlórien mellyrn grew from nuts Tar-Aldarion gave Gil-galad, who gave them to Galadriel.

where: the Nísimaldar about Eldalondë in Númenor; nowhere else in the Land of Gift grew it so tall
q: Its bark was silver and smooth, and its boughs somewhat upswept after the manner of the beech; but it never grew save with a single trunk.
note: Kept separate from the Lothlórien mallorn entry because this is the only botanically detailed passage Tolkien wrote for the tree; the Númenórean trees 'reached after five centuries a height scarce less than it achieved in Eressëa itself', and the Lórien groves 'did not reach the height or girth of the great groves of Númenor'.

where: Cerin Amroth and the lawns of Lothlórien; the Field of Cormallen has no elanor recorded
use: Sam named his eldest daughter for it
q: Well, Sam, what about elanor, the sun-star, you remember the little golden flower in the grass of Lothlórien?
note: Haldir: 'Here ever bloom the winter flowers in the unfading grass: the yellow elanor, and the pale niphredil.' In Letter 312 Tolkien wrote that elanor lit by the light of Faërie 'would be a pimpernel (perhaps a little enlarged) growing sun-golden flowers and star-silver ones on the same plant, and sometimes the two combined' — the only botanical gloss he ever gave it. He called elanor and niphredil 'my best inventions' in a 1944 letter to Christopher.

where: Neldoreth in Doriath, where it first bloomed; Cerin Amroth in Lothlórien
q: and there in the forest of Neldoreth Lúthien was born, and the white flowers of niphredil came forth to greet her as stars from the earth.
note: The Silmarillion index: 'A white flower that bloomed in Doriath in starlight when Lúthien was born.' In Letter 312 Tolkien said niphredil 'would be simply a delicate kin of a snowdrop'. With elanor it marks the grave of Arwen on Cerin Amroth: 'elanor and niphredil bloom no more east of the Sea'.

n: the pale flowers of Cerin Amroth
where: the green hillsides of Cerin Amroth in Lothlórien, among the elanor
q: Among them, nodding on slender stalks, were other flowers, white and palest green: they glimmered as a mist amid the rich hue of the grass.
note: DESCRIBED BUT NOT NAMED — the inverse of the usual gap. Frodo sees them beside the elanor; they are never identified with niphredil, though Haldir names elanor and niphredil as the two flowers of Cerin Amroth in the same chapter, and readers have long assumed the identification. The corpus does not make it.

elv: alfirin, uilos (per UT); simbelmynë is Rohirric (rendered from Old English)
where: the barrows of the Kings below Edoras; the ravine of Gondolin; the mound of Elendil on Amon Anwar
use: grave-flower of the Mark
q: Evermind they are called, simbelmynë in this land of Men, for they blossom in all the seasons of the year, and grow where dead men rest.
note: Gandalf speaks. Tolkien wrote in 1944 that he liked 'A-S symbelmynë or evermind found on the Great Mound of Rohan'. On Helm's mound 'Ever after the white simbelmynë grew there most thickly, so that the mound seemed to be snow-clad.' UT identifies simbelmynë, alfirin and uilos as one flower — see the alfirin entry for the conflict this creates.

where: the great ravine of Gondolin, seen by Tuor in the Elder Days
q: uilos A small white flower, also called alfirin and simbelmynë (Evermind).
note: The quotation is Christopher Tolkien's index entry, not his father's narrative; in the tale of Tuor the flower is described but the element uilos is discussed only in a note ('the word is found also in Amon Uilos, as the Quenya name Oiolossë was rendered into Sindarin'). Included because uilos is the third of the three names UT equates.

where: the mound on Amon Anwar (white); the green fields of Lebennin (golden bells)
q: Alfirin ('immortal') would be an immortelle, but not dry and papery: simply a beautiful bell-like flower, running through many colours, but soft and gentle.
note: CONTESTED. UT's index says 'alfirin A small white flower, also called uilos and simbelmynë (Evermind)', and Cirion sees 'the white flowers of alfirin' on Elendil's mound. But Christopher Tolkien flags the clash himself: 'Alfirin is named, but apparently of a different flower, in a verse that Legolas sang in Minas Tirith: The golden bells are shaken of mallos and alfirin / In the green fields of Lebennin.' White grave-flower and golden bell-flower are not reconciled anywhere in the corpus.

where: the green fields of Lebennin in Gondor
q: And the golden bells are shaken of mallos and alfirin In the green fields of Lebennin, In the wind from the Sea!
note: From Legolas's song of the Sea. UT's index adds only 'mallos A golden flower of Lebennin.' Tolkien listed mallos among the flowers botanists wanted described and never described it further.

elv: asëa aranion (Quenya, 'leaf of kings'); athelas (Sindarin); kingsfoil and westmansweed in Westron
where: grows sparsely, only near places where the Men of the West dwelt or camped of old; found in the woods of Lossarnach and near Weathertop
use: healing, above all of the Black Breath; virtue released in the hands of the king
q: It is fortunate that I could find it, for it is a healing plant that the Men of the West brought to Middle-earth.
note: Aragorn speaks. The Gondorian herb-master gives all three registers: 'kingsfoil, as the rustics name it, or athelas in the noble tongue, or to those who know somewhat of the Valinorean...' — Aragorn cuts him off with 'asëa aranion'. Its scent is described three separate ways: 'a sweet and pungent fragrance', 'like the scent of orchards, and of heather in the sunshine full of bees', and Ioreth's 'wholesome, maybe, is nearer'.

where: Tol Eressëa
use: brought by the Elves to crown guests at the betrothal feast of Aldarion and Erendis
q: and sweet lissuin whose fragrance brings heart's ease.
note: UT's index: 'lissuin A fragrant flower of Tol Eressëa.' This is the whole of what is known of it. The quotation is given from mid-sentence because a footnote marker stands inside the full clause ('were crowned with elanor 20 and sweet lissuin'); the flowers were brought by the Elves of Eressëa to the betrothal feast of Aldarion and Erendis.

where: the bare grey crown of Amon Rûdh in Beleriand
q: Seregon, 'blood of stone', was a plant of the kind called in English 'stonecrop'; it had flowers of a deep red.
c: UT I.2, Narn i Hîn Húrin, note 14
note: The gloss is Christopher Tolkien's note; his father's narrative sentence is 'its steep grey head was bare, save for the red seregon that mantled the stone', and when the sun struck it Andróg said 'See! There is blood on the hill-top.' At the fall of Bar-en-Danwedh the omen is fulfilled: the defenders' 'blood flowed out upon the seregon that mantled the stone'.

where: the lower slopes of Amon Rûdh
q: Aeglos, 'snowthorn', is said to have been like furze (gorse), but larger, and with white flowers.
c: UT I.2, Narn i Hîn Húrin, note 14
note: The narrative gives 'aisles of long-legged aeglos: a gloom filled with a sweet scent'. The same word names the spear of Gil-galad — UT's index lists them as two entries, '*aeglos (1) A plant that grew on Amon Rûdh' and 'Aeglos (2) The spear of Gil-galad (as a word-formation the same as the preceding)'.

where: brought to Númenor by the Eldar of Eressëa; grew in the Nísimaldar
use: the Green Bough of Return, set on the prow of a departing Númenórean ship in token of friendship with Ossë and Uinen
q: The leaves of that tree were ever-green, glossy and fragrant; and it throve upon sea-air.
note: The bough is a portent as much as a custom: when Aldarion's ship came home from the northern cold he found the bough withered, 'for such a thing had never befallen the bough of oiolairë, so long as it was washed with the spray'. On his last voyage as King he set no bough at all, but an eagle of Círdan's making. Corollairë, the Green Mound of the Two Trees in Valinor, was also called Coron Oiolairë.

where: the Nísimaldar about Eldalondë in Númenor
note: NAMED BUT NOT DESCRIBED. It appears only in a list of the fragrant trees of the Nísimaldar; all that is said of the group is that 'Flower, leaf, and rind of those trees exuded sweet scents'.

where: the Nísimaldar about Eldalondë in Númenor
note: NAMED BUT NOT DESCRIBED. Listed among the fragrant trees only; the name is not glossed anywhere in the corpus.

where: the Nísimaldar about Eldalondë in Númenor
note: NAMED BUT NOT DESCRIBED. Listed among the fragrant trees only.

where: the Nísimaldar about Eldalondë in Númenor
note: NAMED BUT NOT DESCRIBED. Listed among the fragrant trees only; the name contains Taniquetil.

where: the Nísimaldar about Eldalondë in Númenor
q: oiolairë and lairelossë, nessamelda, vardarianna, taniquelassë, and yavannamírë with its globed and scarlet fruits.
note: Alone of the Nísimaldar trees besides oiolairë and malinornë it gets a descriptive phrase, and only for its fruit. Nothing is said of its leaf, bark or flower.

elv: Telperion; also Silpion, Ninquelótë ('White Blossom'), and 'many other names'
where: the mound Ezellohar before the western gate of Valmar in Valinor
use: gave light to Valinor; its last flower became the Moon (Isil)
q: The one had leaves of dark green that beneath were as shining silver, and from each of his countless flowers a dew of silver light was ever falling, and the earth beneath was dappled with the shadows of his fluttering leaves.
note: The elder of the Two Trees; his first shining, 'the white glimmer of a silver dawn', was the Opening Hour from which the Valar counted their reign. Poisoned by Ungoliant at Melkor's bidding. Called 'the Eldest of Trees'. In the earliest tradition (Book of Lost Tales) his name was Silpion; Christopher Tolkien notes 'The Two Trees, Silpion (later Telperion) and Laurelin'.

elv: Laurelin; also Malinalda, Culúrien, 'and many names in song beside'
where: the mound Ezellohar before the western gate of Valmar in Valinor
use: gave light to Valinor; its last fruit became the Sun (Anar)
q: The other bore leaves of a young green like the new-opened beech; their edges were of glittering gold.
note: Continues: 'Flowers swung upon her branches in clusters of yellow flame, formed each to a glowing horn that spilled a golden rain upon the ground.' Varda hoarded the dews of Telperion and the rain of Laurelin 'in great vats like shining lakes'. Both Trees were drained by Ungoliant; 'their lifeless stems stand yet in Valinor, a memorial of vanished joy'.

where: the courts of Tirion upon Túna, beneath the Mindon
q: Yavanna made for them a tree like to a lesser image of Telperion, save that it did not give light of its own being; Galathilion it was named in the Sindarin tongue.
note: The first of the White Trees of the line that runs Telperion > Galathilion > Celeborn of Tol Eressëa > Nimloth of Númenor > the White Tree of Minas Ithil and of Minas Tirith.

where: Tol Eressëa
q: And a seedling they brought of Celeborn, the White Tree that grew in the midst of Eressëa; and that was in its turn a seedling of Galathilion the Tree of Túna, the image of Telperion that Yavanna gave to the Eldar in the Blessed Realm.
note: Not to be confused with Celeborn of Lothlórien. The tree is never described apart from its descent.

elv: Nimloth ('White Blossom', Sindarin); = Quenya Ninquelótë, itself a name of Telperion
where: the courts of the King at Armenelos in Númenor
use: memorial of the Eldar and of the light of Valinor; the fortunes of the King's house were held to be bound up with it
q: Nimloth it was named, and flowered in the evening, and the shadows of night it filled with its fragrance.
note: Felled at Sauron's urging and burned as the first fire on the altar of Morgoth: 'the first fire upon the altar was kindled with the hewn wood of Nimloth, and it crackled and was consumed'. Isildur stole a fruit before the felling, and from it descended the White Tree of Gondor.

where: the Court of the Fountain in Minas Tirith; the sapling was found on a hallow high on Mindolluin
use: emblem of the Kings; seven stars and a crown above it on the standard
q: Already it had put forth young leaves long and shapely, dark above and silver beneath, and upon its slender crown it bore one small cluster of flowers whose white petals shone like the sunlit snow.
note: Gandalf on its descent: 'Verily this is a sapling of the line of Nimloth the fair; and that was a seedling of Galathilion, and that a fruit of Telperion of many names, Eldest of Trees.' Aragorn found it 25 June 3019; the withered tree it replaced was laid to rest in Rath Dínen unburnt. Isildur had 'planted there the last sapling of the White Tree in memory of his brother'.

where: Gondor; the Men of the White Mountains use staves of it
use: staves; the casket of black lebethron in which the White Rod of the Stewards was borne
q: They are made of the fair tree lebethron, beloved of the woodwrights of Gondor, and a virtue has been set upon them of finding and returning.
note: Faramir speaks, giving the hobbits staves at Henneth Annûn. The wood is elsewhere called black. The tree itself is never described — only its timber and the virtue set upon it.

where: the Field of Cormallen in Ithilien
q: Cormallen, which means 'golden circle' and was named from the culumalda trees that grew there (see cul-).
c: Silm, Appendix: Elements in Quenya and Sindarin names
note: The name occurs nowhere in the narrative — only in the Silmarillion's name-elements appendix. The trees of Cormallen ARE described in LotR VI.4 as 'stately dark-leaved trees laden with scarlet blossom', but there they are not named culumalda; the identification is an inference the corpus permits but does not state.

where: first truly grown at Longbottom in the Southfarthing; grows well on the south slopes of the hill at Bree; in Gondor esteemed only for its flowers
use: smoked in pipes of clay or wood — 'Hobbits first put it into pipes'
q: they imbibed or inhaled, through pipes of clay or wood, the smoke of the burning leaves of a herb, which they called pipe-weed or leaf, a variety probably of Nicotiana.
note: Merry Brandybuck's Herblore of the Shire is the authority. Gondorian names: 'it is called westmansweed by the vulgar, and galenas by the noble, and other names in other tongues more learned'. Tobold Hornblower of Longbottom first grew it c. S.R. 1070 (T.A. 2670); 'How Old Toby came by the plant is not recorded, for to his dying day he would not tell.'

where: Gondor, where it was not smoked
use: esteemed only for the fragrance of its flowers
q: The Men of Gondor call it sweet galenas, and esteem it only for the fragrance of its flowers.
note: Same plant as pipe-weed, kept separate because the corpus keeps the registers separate (LotR index: 'Galenas see Pipe-weed'). Gandalf's parody of the herb-master supplies the third name: 'it is called westmansweed by the vulgar, and galenas by the noble'.

n: Longbottom Leaf, Old Toby, Southern Star
where: the Southfarthing of the Shire, chiefly about Longbottom
use: the three named cultivars of pipe-weed
q: The best home-grown still comes from that district, especially the varieties now known as Longbottom Leaf, Old Toby, and Southern Star.
note: Only these three varieties are ever named. Merry found Hornblower-branded barrels of Longbottom Leaf in the wreck of Isengard — 'as fine a pipe-weed as you could wish for, and quite unspoilt' — which Aragorn found the most curious fact of the campaign: 'leaf from the Southfarthing in Isengard.'

elv: kelvar 'animals, living things that move'; olvar 'growing things with roots in the earth'
where: all Arda
q: But the kelvar can flee or defend themselves, whereas the olvar that grow cannot.
note: Yavanna's own division of living things, and the root of the whole matter: her plea for the olvar is what brings the Shepherds of the Trees into the world. Manwë answers that when the Children awake, spirits will 'go among the kelvar and the olvar, and some will dwell therein, and be held in reverence, and their just anger shall be feared'.

pl: mearas is itself the plural (Old English mearh, pl. mearas)
where: the Mark of Rohan; their sire came 'from West over Sea'
use: steeds of the Kings of the Mark alone
q: These were the mearas, who would bear no one but the King of the Mark or his sons, until the time of Shadowfax.
note: Descended from Felaróf, the horse of Eorl, who 'understood all that men said, and was as long-lived as they'. Their origin is legend, not record: 'Men said of them that Béma (whom the Eldar call Oromë) must have brought their sire from West over Sea.' In The Treason of Isengard an earlier draft made their sires come 'out of the Lost Land over the Great Sea when the Kings of Men came out of the Deeps to Gondor' — i.e. Númenórean, not Valinorean, stock; the published text changed this.

where: east of the Misty Mountains 'over the Edge of the Wild'; also west of the Mountains in T.A. 3019
use: ridden by Orcs — 'Sometimes they rode on wolves like men do on horses'
q: But even the wild Wargs (for so the evil wolves over the Edge of the Wild were named) cannot climb trees.
note: They have a language of their own — 'He spoke to them in the dreadful language of the Wargs' — and hold councils. Whether Wargs are simply wolves or something more is never settled: Aragorn's proverb is 'where the warg howls, there also the orc prowls', and after the fight at Hollin the slain bodies were gone by morning.

where: the North; they came into Eriador and the Shire over the frozen Brandywine in the Fell Winter of T.A. 2911 (S.R. 1311)
q: No living hobbit (save Bilbo) could remember the Fell Winter of 1311, when white wolves invaded the Shire over the frozen Brandywine.
note: Named but never described beyond their colour and the fact of the invasion; the Tale of Years records only 'White Wolves invade Eriador from the North.' The Horn-call of Buckland was last sounded for them.

where: Angband; Tol-in-Gaurhoth in Beleriand
use: Sauron's beasts; he took their form himself
q: Then Sauron sent Draugluin, a dread beast, old in evil, lord and sire of the werewolves of Angband.
note: Sauron is 'lord of werewolves'; to fight Huan he 'took upon himself the form of a werewolf, and made himself the mightiest that had yet walked the world'. Carcharoth was bred from Draugluin's whelps, fed by Morgoth's own hand on living flesh, until 'the fire and anguish of hell entered into him'. The corpus never states what stock werewolves were bred from.

n: Huan and the hounds of Oromë
where: Valinor; afterwards Beleriand
use: the hunt
q: Huan The great wolfhound of Valinor that Oromë gave to Celegorm.
note: Quotation is from the Silmarillion index. Huan is a single beast rather than a kind, but he is the only member of the hounds of Valinor ever named, and Oromë 'delights in horses and in hounds'. It was decreed he should meet death only from the mightiest wolf that should ever walk the world.

pl: crebain is the plural form used; no singular is given in the corpus
where: Fangorn and Dunland; they flew over Hollin in T.A. 3019
use: spies
q: They are not natives here; they are crebain out of Fangorn and Dunland.
note: Aragorn speaks. Described only as 'regiments of black crows'; Christopher Tolkien's index to The Treason of Isengard glosses them simply 'Crows seen over Hollin'. No singular form and no Elvish gloss of the name occurs anywhere in the corpus — do not assume one.

where: the Harad; brought north to Ithilien and the Pelennor
use: war-beasts of the Haradrim, bearing war-towers
q: the Mûmak of Harad was indeed a beast of vast bulk, and the like of him does not walk now in Middle-earth; his kin that live still in latter days are but memories of his girth and majesty.
note: The plural mûmakil is given only in the LotR index ('Mûmak (pl. mûmakil) see Oliphaunt'). Hobbits call it oliphaunt after an old Shire rhyme — 'Grey as a mouse, / Big as a house' — which Sam recites believing it 'Nonsense maybe, and maybe not' before he sees one. All those at the siege of Gondor were destroyed.

elv: Thorondor ('King of Eagles'); Gwaihir the Windlord; Landroval
where: the Crissaegrim above Gondolin in the Elder Days; the peaks of the Misty Mountains in the Third Age; an eyrie in the tower of the King in Armenelos
use: messengers and rescuers; 'I was sent to bear tidings not burdens'
q: there flew down from the high airs Thorondor, King of Eagles, mightiest of all birds that have ever been, whose outstretched wings spanned thirty fathoms
note: They are Manwë's: 'In the mountains the Eagles shall house, and hear the voices of those who call upon us.' Gwaihir and Landroval are 'greatest of all the Eagles of the North, mightiest of the descendants of old Thorondor'. In Númenor 'the great eagles that were held sacred to Manwë, and never afflicted, until the days of evil and the hatred of the Valar began' kept an eyrie on the King's palace for two thousand years.

elv: Onodrim, Enyd (Sindarin); Ent is the Rohirric form
pl: Enyd (Sindarin plural of onod); Onodrim as a collective
where: Fangorn Forest; formerly all the great woods of the Elder Days
q: The most ancient people surviving in the Third Age were the Onodrim or Enyd.
note: Gandalf calls them 'the shepherds of the trees'; the Silmarillion has Manwë promise Yavanna 'in the forests shall walk the Shepherds of the Trees'. Christopher Tolkien records that in constructing the published Silmarillion the Ents were introduced into the sack of Nogrod from his father's 1963 letter, and that 'This story was not lightly or easily conceived' — the Ents' place in the First Age is editorial reconstruction, not settled text. They ascribed to the Eldar not their language but the desire for speech.

where: once the gardens east of Anduin, now the Brown Lands; whereabouts unknown
q: But the Entwives gave their minds to the lesser trees, and to the meads in the sunshine beyond the feet of the forests
note: Treebeard is careful about the verb: 'They did not die! I never said died. We lost them, I said. We lost them and we cannot find them.' Their fate is deliberately left open — 'some said that they had seen them walking away west, and some said east, and others south'. Fimbrethil (Wandlimb) is the only Entwife named. Because they are lost there have been no Entings 'for a terrible long count of years'.

where: the darkest dales of Fangorn; they came to Helm's Deep and Isengard
q: I think they are Ents that have become almost like trees, at least to look at.
note: CONTESTED BY DESIGN. Merry reports the word as the Ents' own 'short language' and admits 'Treebeard won't say much about them'; the whole account is a hobbit's guess. Whether a Huorn is an Ent gone tree-ish or a tree gone Ent-ish is never settled: 'They still have voices, and can speak with the Ents – that is why they are called Huorns, Treebeard says – but they have become queer and wild.' Treebeard himself says of Saruman only that 'His heart is as rotten as a black Huorn's.'

elv: Araw is the Sindarin name of Oromë
where: the far fields of Rhûn, near the Sea of Rhûn
use: Vorondil hunted them; the Horn of Gondor was made from one
q: The wild white kine that were still to be found near the Sea of Rhûn were said in legend to be descended from the Kine of Araw, the huntsman of the Valar, who alone of the Valar came often to Middle-earth in the Elder Days.
note: Carefully hedged: 'were said in legend to be descended from'. Boromir's horn was 'the eldest son of our house, far back into the vanished years... since Vorondil father of Mardil hunted the wild kine of Araw'.

where: Valinor and the wilds of Middle-earth
use: the horse of Oromë
q: Nahar is the name of his horse, white in the sun, and shining silver at night.
note: His neighing at Cuiviénen is what brought the Valar to the Elves: 'Then on a sudden Nahar set up a great neighing, and stood still.' Elsewhere 'his white horse shod with gold'. A single beast, not a kind, but the fountainhead of the horse-lore of Arda.

where: the shores of Eldamar; Ossë's gift
use: they drew the ships of the Teleri over the sea; the Telerin ships were made in their likeness
q: Then the swans drew the white ships of the Teleri over the windless sea; and thus at last and latest they came to Aman and the shores of Eldamar.
note: Ossë gave them as a parting gift when he taught the Teleri ship-building. Their ships 'were made in the likeness of swans, with beaks of gold and eyes of gold and jet'. Seven great swans flying south are the sign to Tuor that he has tarried too long in Nevrast.

n: the fell beasts of the Nazgûl
where: bred in some 'hideous eyrie' in 'forgotten mountains cold beneath the Moon'
use: winged steeds of the Ringwraiths from T.A. 3019
q: if bird, then greater than all other birds, and it was naked, and neither quill nor feather did it bear, and its vast pinions were as webs of hide between horned fingers; and it stank.
note: They are never given a name of their own — 'fell beast', 'winged creature', 'winged steed' are all the text offers, and even the kind is left doubtful ('if bird, then...'). Their origin is stated as a guess: 'A creature of an older world maybe it was, whose kind, lingering in forgotten mountains cold beneath the Moon, outstayed their day, and in hideous eyrie bred this last untimely brood, apt to evil.' Frodo compares them to 'great carrion birds'.

elv: Torech Ungol is her lair, 'Shelob's Lair'
where: Torech Ungol under the Ephel Dúath, above Cirith Ungol
q: Most like a spider she was, but huger than the great hunting beasts, and more terrible than they because of the evil purpose in her remorseless eyes.
note: 'Most like a spider' — the text stops short of calling her one. 'Shelob the Great, last child of Ungoliant to trouble the unhappy world'; she 'served none but herself... for all living things were her food, and her vomit darkness'. Sauron 'his cat he calls her, but she owns him not'. She had 'no softer spot... save only her eyes'.

where: from the Ephel Dúath to the eastern hills, to Dol Guldur and the fastnesses of Mirkwood
q: Far and wide her lesser broods, bastards of the miserable mates, her own offspring, that she slew, spread from glen to glen, from the Ephel Dúath to the eastern hills, to Dol Guldur and the fastnesses of Mirkwood.
note: This is the corpus's only link between Shelob and the spiders of Mirkwood, and it is a link of descent, not identity ('lesser broods'). She kills her mates and her own offspring.

where: the black heart of Mirkwood, about the Elvenking's realm
q: With that one of the fat spiders ran along a rope till it came to a dozen bundles hanging in a row from a high branch.
note: They speak: 'Aye, they'll make fine eating, when they've hung a bit.' Bilbo names them 'Attercop' and 'Lazy Lob' in his taunting songs. Their bodily form is nowhere set out — only their fatness, their webs, and their poison, which they use 'as small spiders do to flies'. Distinguish them from the ordinary small spiders Bilbo found in the treetops chasing butterflies.

elv: Ungoliant; Ungwë Lianti in the earliest tradition
where: Avathar, in the shadows between the Pelóri and the sea; afterwards Nan Dungortheb
q: But she had disowned her Master, desiring to be mistress of her own lust, taking all things to herself to feed her emptiness; and she fled to the south
note: CONTESTED ORIGIN, and the text says so: 'The Eldar knew not whence she came; but some have said that in ages long before she descended from the darkness that lies about Arda... and that in the beginning she was one of those that he corrupted to his service.' Her end is likewise uncertain in the corpus. She drank the light of the Two Trees and 'belched forth black vapours as she drank'.

where: Sauron's isle of Tol-in-Gaurhoth; she flew to Angband
use: messenger of Sauron; her bat-fell was worn by Lúthien
q: She was the messenger of Sauron, and was wont to fly in vampire's form to Angband; and her great fingered wings were barbed at each joint's end with an iron claw.
note: The only vampire named in the published Silmarillion. Whether 'vampire' names a kind or only a shape she took is not stated.

where: Angband; afterwards Withered Heath and the North
q: Again after a hundred years Glaurung, the first of the Urulóki, the fire-drakes of the North, issued from Angband's gates by night.
note: The same passage gives the one general statement about the kind: 'He was yet young and scarce half-grown, for long and slow is the life of the dragons.' Urulóki is glossed in the Silmarillion index simply as 'Quenya word meaning fire-serpent, dragon' — so it is not certain the term is restricted to the wingless fire-drakes.

where: the pits of Angband; loosed in the War of Wrath
q: out of the pits of Angband there issued the winged dragons, that had not before been seen; and so sudden and ruinous was the onset of that dreadful fleet that the host of the Valar was driven back
note: 'the coming of the dragons was with great thunder, and lightning, and a tempest of fire'. Ancalagon the Black is 'Greatest of the winged dragons of Morgoth, destroyed by Eärendil'. Whether the winged dragons are a distinct breed or a later form of the Urulóki is not stated.

where: the Grey Mountains and the Withered Heath
q: At last Dáin I, together with Frór his second son, was slain at the doors of his hall by a great cold-drake.
note: NAMED BUT NOT DESCRIBED. This single sentence is the only occurrence of the word 'cold-drake' in the primary corpus. Nothing is said of what makes a cold-drake cold, nor whether it is winged; the contrast with the fire-drakes (Urulóki) is implied by the name alone.

where: the North; the Withered Heath beyond the Grey Mountains
q: There were lots of dragons in the North in those days, and gold was probably getting scarce up there, with the dwarves flying south or getting killed, and all the general waste and destruction that dragons make going from bad to worse.
note: Thorin's account. Also from the same passage, on dragons as craftsmen: they 'make a thing for themselves, not even mend a little loose scale of their armour' — they hoard but cannot make. Smaug is called 'a most specially greedy, strong and wicked worm'.

where: the Trollshaws and the Ettenmoors (Stone-trolls); Moria (cave-troll); the mountains (Hill-trolls, Snow-trolls)
q: In their beginning far back in the twilight of the Elder Days, these were creatures of dull and lumpish nature and had no more language than beasts.
note: Sauron 'made use of them, teaching them what little they could learn and increasing their wits with wickedness'. The Stone-trolls of the Westlands spoke a debased Common Speech. The older race turned to stone in the sun — Bilbo's three trolls do. What Trolls were made FROM is never stated in Appendix F; in Morgoth's Ring Tolkien reconsidered whether such creatures could be independent beings at all.

where: southern Mirkwood and the mountain borders of Mordor, at the end of the Third Age
q: Trolls they were, but filled with the evil will of their master: a fell race, strong, agile, fierce and cunning, but harder than stone.
note: CONTESTED WITHIN THE TEXT ITSELF: 'That Sauron bred them none doubted, though from what stock was not known. Some held that they were not Trolls but giant Orcs; but the Olog-hai were in fashion of body and mind quite unlike even the largest of Orc-kind.' Unlike the older race they could endure the Sun 'so long as the will of Sauron held sway over them'. In Morgoth's Ring Christopher Tolkien notes his father later linked this passage to the question of Men-derived Orcs.

elv: orch (Sindarin), pl. yrch; uruk (Black Speech) for the great soldier-orcs; snaga 'slave' for the lesser kinds
where: Angband and Utumno in the Elder Days; the Misty Mountains, Mordor, Isengard, Mirkwood
q: The Orcs were first bred by the Dark Power of the North in the Elder Days.
note: THE MOST CONTESTED KIND IN THE LEGENDARIUM, and Tolkien never settled it. The published Silmarillion states the Avari theory: Melkor bred them from Quendi taken captive, 'in envy and mockery of the Elves'. But in Morgoth's Ring Christopher Tolkien tracks at least four positions: (1) Orcs 'made of stone', Morgoth's own creation; (2) 'a spawn of earth corrupted by Morgoth'; (3) the 'darker tale... told in Eressëa' of corrupted Avari; (4) a late note that 'The Orcs were beasts of humanized shape (to mock Men and Elves)', whose talking 'was really reeling off records set in them by Melkor'; and a still later view that they were Elvish in origin but that 'later some were probably derived from Men'. The single fixed point Tolkien kept returning to is Frodo's: 'The Shadow that bred them can only mock, it cannot make: not real new things of its own.'

where: the dammed lake before the West-gate of Moria
q: Out from the water a long sinuous tentacle had crawled; it was pale-green and luminous and wet.
note: Its kind is never stated and it is never seen whole — only 'twenty other arms' and 'many coiling arms'. Its name comes from Ori's last entry in the Book of Mazarbul: 'the pool is up to the wall at Westgate. The Watcher in the Water took Óin.' Gandalf says later only that 'Something has crept, or has been driven out of dark waters under the mountains. There are older and fouler things than Orcs in the deep places of the world.'

where: Ravenhill and the Lonely Mountain
use: messengers and counsellors of the Kings under the Mountain
q: They live many a year, and their memories are long, and they hand on their wisdom to their children.
note: Thorin distinguishes them sharply from crows: 'Those were crows! And nasty suspicious-looking creatures at that, and rude as well... But the ravens are different.' Roäc son of Carc, their chief, was 153 years old and spoke the Common Speech. They took payment in bright things.

where: the Lonely Mountain and Dale
use: knocked at the keyhole stone; told Bard where Smaug was unarmoured
q: Unafraid it perched by his ear and it brought him news.
note: 'Marvelling he found he could understand its tongue, for he was of the race of Dale.' The thrushes of Dale are a long-lived, speaking kind but are never given a name; the old thrush is described only as 'watching from a high perch with beady eyes and head cocked on one side'.

where: Númenor
q: the kirinki that were no bigger than wrens, but all scarlet, with piping voices on the edge of human hearing
note: The smallest of the birds of Númenor, set against 'the great eagles that were held sacred to Manwë' at the other end of the same sentence.

where: the black heart of Mirkwood, drawn by torchlight
q: Worse still it brought thousands of dark-grey and black moths, some nearly as big as your hand, flapping and whirring round their ears.
note: Named only as a plague of the forest. The moth that warns Gandalf at Orthanc is not in Tolkien's text and has no source in this corpus.

where: the sunlit roof of leaves above Mirkwood
q: I expect they were a kind of 'purple emperor', a butterfly that loves the tops of oak-woods, but these were not purple at all, they were a dark dark velvety black without any markings to be seen.
note: The narrator hedges the identification ('I expect they were a kind of'). Bilbo alone of the company saw them, from the treetop. In The Fall of Gondolin it is said that 'all butterflies and their kindred were born in the valley of the Land of Willows'.

n: the fish of the Forest River
where: the Forest River of Mirkwood, and the Long Lake into which it flows
note: NAMED BUT NOT DESCRIBED — indeed barely named. Despite the Forest River being travelled at length in Barrels Out of Bond, no fish of it is ever mentioned; the only fish in The Hobbit are Gollum's blind fish of the underground lake ('fish whose fathers swam in, goodness only knows how many years ago') and the Lake-men's proverbial 'poisoned fish' that Bard was mocked for foreboding. The absence is worth recording: Tolkien named the trees, birds, moths and butterflies of Mirkwood but never its fish.

NOT A PLANT. The only occurrence of 'mirian' in the primary corpus is in The Peoples of Middle-earth, where it is a Gondorian silver coin ('part of the mirian'), not a flower. Recorded here so the mistake is not repeated: no plant of this name exists in Tolkien.

where: Valinor and the woods of Oromë
q: and he though grieving taught them the craft of ship-building; and when their ships were built he brought them as his parting gift many strong-winged swans.
note: Tolkien never gives a bestiary of Valinor. What the corpus offers is scattered: Oromë's horse Nahar and his hounds; the swans of Ossë; Manwë's eagles; Yavanna's kelvar generally. Oromë alone of the Valar 'came often to Middle-earth in the Elder Days', 'pursuing to the death the monsters and fell creatures of the kingdom of Melkor'. The quotation stands here for the one gift of living beasts out of Valinor that the text records in full.
